input-and-type-conversion.label: Input and Type Conversion
input-and-type-conversion.summary: Use input, int, float, and str to handle user-entered values.

sketch1.title: Understanding Input and Type Conversion
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
In Python, you can use the `input()` function to get user input. By default, this input is a string. To use it as a number, you need to convert it using `int()` or `float()`. 

Example:

```python
user_input = input('Enter a number: ')
number = int(user_input)
print('You entered:', number)
```

This code asks the user for a number, converts it to an integer, and prints it. 

Try it yourself: Modify the code to handle a floating-point number instead.

Line by line: read the first statement to see which value is stored or checked, then follow each conditional branch in order to see which path prints the final result.

sketch2.title: Converting Types
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Type conversion is essential when you need to perform operations on user input. Use `int()` to convert a string to an integer, `float()` for a floating-point number, and `str()` to convert numbers back to strings.

Example:

```python
age = input('Enter your age: ')
age_in_5_years = int(age) + 5
print('In 5 years, you will be', age_in_5_years)
```

This code calculates how old you will be in 5 years. 

Your turn: Change the code to calculate your age in 10 years.

Line by line: read the first statement to see which value is stored or checked, then follow each conditional branch in order to see which path prints the final result.
